title: INTERPOLATION
body: «TITLE:«B»INTERPOLATION«/B»«/TITLE»PAGE:706(from Lat. «I»interpolare«/I», to alter, or insert something fresh, connected with «I»polire«/I», a polish), in mathematics, the process of obtaining intermediate terms of a series of which particular terms only are given. The cubes, for instance, shown in the second column of the accompanying table, may be regarded as terms of a series, and the cube of a fractional number, not exceeding the last number in the first column, may be found by interpolation. The process of obtaining the cube of a number exceeding the last number in the first column would be «I»extrapolation«/I»; the formulae which apply to interpolation apply in theory to extrapolation, but in practice special precautions as to accuracy are necessary. The present article deals only with interpolation.«P»«TABLE[cols:2|class:data-table|rules:cols|style:font-size:92%;line-height:130%;border:1px solid;border-collapse:collapse;margin-right:auto;margin-left:auto]»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD[style:padding-left:0.5em;padding-right:0.5em;border:1px solid]»Number.«/TD»«TD[style:padding-left:0.5em;padding-right:0.5em;border:1px solid]»Cube of Number.«/TD»«/TR»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD»0«/TD»«TD»0«/TD»«/TR»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD»1«/TD»«TD»1«/TD»«/TR»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD»2«/TD»«TD»8«/TD»«/TR»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD»3«/TD»«TD»27«/TD»«/TR»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD»4«/TD»«TD»64«/TD»«/TR»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD»5«/TD»«TD»125«/TD»«/TR»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD»6«/TD»«TD»216«/TD»«/TR»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD».«/TD»«TD».«/TD»«/TR»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD».«/TD»«TD».«/TD»«/TR»«TR[style:text-align:center]»«TD».«/TD»«TD».«/TD»«/TR»«/TABLE»«P»The term is usually limited to those cases in which there are two quantities, «I»x«/I» and «I»u«/I», which are so related that when «I»x«/I» has any arbitrary value, lying perhaps between certain limits, the value of «I»u«/I» is determinate. There is a given series of associated values of «I»u«/I» and of «I»x«/I», and interpolation consists in determining the value of «I»u«/I» for any arbitrary value of «I»x«/I», or the value of «I»x«/I» for any arbitrary value of «I»u«/I», lying between two of the values in the series. Either of the two quantities may be regarded as a function of the other; it is convenient to treat one, «I»x«/I», as the “independent variable,” the other, «I»u«/I», being treated as the “dependent variable,” «I»i.e.«/I» as a function of «I»x«/I». If, as is usually the case, the successive values of one of the quantities proceed by a constant increment, this quantity is to be regarded as the independent variable. The two series of values may be tabulated, those of «I»x«/I» being placed in a column (or row), and those of «I»u«/I» in a parallel column (or row); «I»u«/I» is then said to be «I»tabulated in terms of x«/I». The independent variable «I»x«/I» is called the «I»argument«/I», and the dependent variable «I»u«/I» is called the «I»entry«/I». Interpolation, in the ordinary sense, consists in determining the value of «I»u«/I» for a value of «I»x«/I» intermediate between two values appearing in the table. This may be described as «I»direct interpolation«/I», to distinguish it from «I»inverse interpolation«/I», which consists in determining the value of «I»x«/I» for a value of «I»u«/I» intermediate between two in the table. The methods employed can be extended to cases in which the value of «I»u«/I» depends on the values of two or more independent quantities «I»x«/I», «I»y«/I»,...«P»«DIV[style:font-size:83%]» In the ordinary case we may regard the values of «I»x«/I» as measured along a straight line OX from a fixed point O, so that to any value of «I»x«/I» there corresponds a point on the line. If we represent the corresponding value of «I»u«/I» by an ordinate drawn from the line, the extremities of all such ordinates will lie on a curve which will be the graph of «I»u«/I» with regard to «I»x«/I». Interpolation therefore consists in determining the length of the ordinate of a curve occupying a particular position, when the lengths of ordinates occupying certain specified positions are known. If «I»u«/I» is a function of two variables, «I»x«/I» and «I»y«/I», we may similarly represent it by the ordinate of a surface, the position of the ordinate being determined by the values of «I»x«/I» and of «I»y«/I» jointly.«P»The series or tables to which interpolation has to be applied may for convenience be regarded as falling into two main groups. The first group comprises mathematical tables, «I»i.e.«/I» tables of mathematical functions; in the case of such a table the value of the function u for each tabulated value of «I»x«/I» is calculated to a known degree of accuracy, and the degree of accuracy of an interpolated value of u can be estimated. The second group comprises tables of values which are found experimentally, «I»e.g.«/I» values of a physical quantity or of a statistical ratio; these values are usually subject to certain “errors” of observation or of random selection (see «LN:22-0390-probability-PROBABILITY.json|Probability|Probability«/LN»). The methods of interpolation are usually the same in the two groups of cases, but special considerations have to be taken into account in the second group. The line of demarcation of the two groups is not absolutely fixed; the tables used by actuaries, for instance, which are of great importance in practical life, are based on statistical observations, but the tables formed directly from the observations have been “smoothed” so as to obtain series which correspond in form to the series of values of mathematical functions.«P»It must be assumed, at any rate in the case of a mathematical function, that the “entry” «I»u«/I» varies continuously with the “argument” «I»x«/I», «I»i.e.«/I» that there are no sudden breaks, changes of direction, &c., in the curve which is the graph of «I»u«/I».«P»Various methods of interpolation are described below. The simplest is that which uses the «I»principle of proportional parts«/I»; and mathematical tables are usually arranged so as to enable this method to be employed. Where this is not possible, the methods are based either on the use of Taylor’s Theorem, which gives a formula involving differential coefficients (see «LN:14-0566-s2-INFINITESIMAL_CALCULUS.json|Infinitesimal Calculus|Infinitesimal Calculus«/LN»), or on the properties of finite differences (see «LN:08-0240-differences-calculus-of-DIFFERENCES__CALCULUS_OF.json|Differences, Calculus of|Differences, Calculus of«/LN»). Taylor’s Theorem can only be applied directly to a known mathematical function; but it can be applied indirectly, by means of finite differences, in various cases where the form of the function expressing «I»u«/I» in terms of «I»x«/I» is unknown; and even where the form of this function is known it is sometimes more convenient to determine the differential coefficients by means of the differences than to calculate them directly from their mathematical expressions. Finally, there are cases where we cannot even employ finite-difference formulae directly. In these cases we must adopt some special method; «I»e.g.«/I» we may instead of «I»u«/I» tabulate some function of «I»u«/I», such as its logarithm, which is found to be amenable to ordinary processes, then determine the value of this function corresponding to the particular value of «I»x«/I», and thence determine the corresponding value of «I»u«/I» itself.«P»In considering methods of interpolation, it will be assumed, unless the contrary is stated, that the values of «I»x«/I» proceed by a constant increment, which will be denoted by «I»h«/I».«P»In order to see what method is to be employed, it is usually necessary to arrange the given series of values of «I»u«/I» in the form of a table, as explained above, and then to take the successive «I»differences«/I» of «I»u«/I». The differences of the successive values of «I»u«/I» are called its «I»first differences«/I»; these form a new series, the first differences of which are the «I»second differences«/I» of «I»u«/I»; and so on. The systems of notation of the differences are explained briefly below. For the fuller discussion, reference should be made to «LN:08-0240-differences-calculus-of-DIFFERENCES__CALCULUS_OF.json|Differences, Calculus of|Differences, Calculus of«/LN».«P»«SEC:interpolation-from-mathematical-tables|I. Direct Interpolation.«/CTR»«P»1. «I»Interpolation by First Differences.«/I»—The simplest cases are those in which the first difference in «I»u«/I» is constant, or nearly so. «I»u«/I» corresponding to a difference of «I»h«/I» ≡ .001 in «I»x«/I» is .0001000; but, since we are working throughout to seven places of decimals, it is more convenient to write it 1000. This system of ignoring the decimal point in dealing with differences will be adopted throughout this article. To find «I»u«/I» for an intermediate value of «I»x«/I» we assume the principle of proportional parts, «I»i.e.«/I» we assume that the difference in «I»u«/I» is proportional to the difference in «I»x«/I». Thus for «I»x«/I» = 4.342945 the difference in «I»u«/I» is .945 of 1000 = 945, so that «I»u«/I» is .6376898 + .0000945 = .6377843. For «I»x«/I» = 4.34294482 the difference in «I»u«/I» would be 944.82, so that the value of «I»u«/I» would apparently be .6376898 + .000094482 = .637784282. This, however, would be incorrect. It must be remembered that the values of «I»u«/I» are only given “correct to seven places of decimals,” «I»i.e.«/I» eachPAGE:707 tabulated value differs from the corresponding true value by a «I»tabular«/I» «I»error«/I» which may have any value up to ± ½ of .0000001; and we cannot therefore by interpolation obtain a result which is correct to nine places. If the interpolated value of «I»u«/I» has to be used in calculations for which it is important that this value should be as accurate as possible, it may be convenient to retain it temporarily in the form .6376898 + 944 82 = .6377842 82 or .6376898 + 944<sup>82</sup> = .6377842<sup>82</sup>; but we must ultimately return to the seven-place arrangement and write it as .6377843. The result of interpolation by first difference is thus usually subject to two inaccuracies, the first being the tabular error of «I»u«/I» itself, and the second being due to the necessity of adjusting the final figure of the added (proportional) difference. If the tabulated values are correct to seven places of decimals, the interpolated value, with the final figure adjusted, will be within .0000001 of its true value.«P»In Example 2 the differences do not at first sight appear to run regularly, but this is only due to the fact that the final figure in each value of «I»u«/I» represents, as explained in the last paragraph, an approximation to the true value. The general principle on which we proceed is the same; but we use the actual difference corresponding to the interval in which the value of «I»x«/I» lies. Thus for «I»x«/I» = 7.41373 we should have «I»u«/I» = .86982 + (.373 of 58) = .87004; this result being correct within .00001.«P»2. «I»Interpolation by Second Differences.«/I»—If the consecutive first differences of «I»u«/I» are not approximately equal, we must take account of the next order of differences. The notation is as follows. The series of values of «I»x«/I» and of «I»u«/I» are respectively «I»x«/I»<sub>0</sub>, «I»x«/I»<sub>1</sub>, «I»x«/I»<sub>2</sub>, ... and «I»u«/I»<sub>0</sub>, «I»u«/I»<sub>1</sub>, «I»u«/I»<sub>2</sub>, ... ; and the successive differences of «I»u«/I» are denoted by Δ«I»u«/I», Δ<sup>2</sup>u, ... It does not follow that these differences should be used for interpolation. Whatever formula is employed, the first difference should always be the tabular first difference, not the corrected first difference; and, further, if a central-difference formula is used, each difference of odd order should be the tabular difference of the corrected differences of the next lower order. (This last result is indirectly achieved if Everett’s formula is used.) With these precautions (i.) the central-difference formula is slightly improved by using corrected instead of tabular differences, and (ii.) the advancing-difference formula is greatly improved, being better than the central-difference formula with tabular differences, but still not so good as the latter with corrected differences. The relation of «I»u«/I» to «I»x«/I» may be such that the successive differences of «I»u«/I» increase rapidly, so that interpolation-formulae cannot be employed directly. Other methods have then to be used. The best method is to replace «I»u«/I» by some expression v which is a function of «I»u«/I» such that (i.) the value of v or of «I»u«/I» can be determined for any given value of «I»u«/I» or of v, and (ii.) when v is tabulated in terms of «I»x«/I» the differences decrease rapidly. We can then calculate v, and thence «I»u«/I», for any intermediate value of «I»x«/I».«P»If, for instance, we require tan «I»x«/I» for a value of «I»x«/I» which is nearly 90°, it will be found that the table of tangents is not suitable for interpolation. We can, however, convert it into a table of cotangents to about the same number of significant figures; from this we can easily calculate cot «I»x«/I», and thence tan «I»x«/I».«P»11. This method is specially suitable for statistical data, where the successive values of «I»u«/I» represent the area of a figure of frequency up to successive ordinates. We have first to determine, by inspection, a curve which bears a general similarity to the unknown curve of frequency, and whose area and abscissa are so related that either can be readily calculated when the other is known. This may be called the «I»auxiliary curve«/I». Denoting by ξ the abscissa of this curve which corresponds to area «I»u«/I», we find the value of ξ corresponding to each of the given values of «I»u«/I». Then, tabulating ξ in terms of «I»x«/I», we have a table in which, if the auxiliary curve has been well chosen, differences of ξ after the first or second are negligible. We can therefore find ξ, and thence «I»u«/I», for any intermediate value of «I»x«/I».«P»«CTR»«I»Extensions.«/I»«/CTR»«P»12. «I»Construction of Formulae.«/I»—Any difference of «I»u«/I» of the «I»r«/I» th order involves «I»r«/I» + 1 consecutive values of «I»u«/I», and it might be expressed by the suffixes which indicate these values.
